event: Born in a country with a high burden of tuberculosis
Positive QuantiFERON gold test
No evidence of TB disease
History of iron deficiency anemia
History of moderately active systemic lupus erythematosus
Medications: naproxen (375 mg, twice daily) and hydroxychloroquine (5.5 mg/kg/d)
Started treatment with 600 mg rifampin (10 mg/kg/d)
Tolerated the medication well for 11 days
Chills and myalgias
Stopped treatment for 3 days
Resumed rifampin
Developed sudden onset severe neck pain, myalgias, arthralgias, shortness of breath and chest pain
Received fluid bolus for hypotension (80/40 mm Hg)
Admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU)
No rash or mucous membrane involvement
Decreased leukocyte count at admission (2.84 × 10^9/L)
Increased leukocyte count to 17.34 × 10^9/L 6 hours later
Low eosinophil counts throughout (0.00 × 10^9/L)
Hemoglobin level decreased to 74 g/L on the second day of admission
Elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 13.6 mg/L at admission
Elevated CRP level of 101.8 mg/L 24 hours later
Increased aspartate aminotransferase level from 71 U/L to 99 U/L
Slightly decreased complement C3 level (0.76 g/L)
Normal C4 level
Normal creatinine kinase level
Increased cortisol level (605 nmol/L)
CT scan of the head and echocardiogram were normal
CT angiography of the neck showed localized myositis
Chest radiograph showed mild pulmonary edema
CT imaging of the lungs showed bilateral increased septal thickening with ground glass opacities
Negative infectious work-up
Negative for SARS-CoV-2 on polymerase chain reaction
Received 1 dose of rifampin in the ICU, which led to a decreased blood pressure of 93/39 mm Hg
Increased vasopressor dose for 6 hours (norepinephrine 0.04–0.06 μg/kg/min)
Stopped rifampin and weaned off vasopressors within 30 hours of admission
Started on isoniazid for TBI
Completed 9 months of therapy
Advised to avoid rifamycin-based medications in the future
